name: Critical Observation
instructions: You will be presented with business requirements, project briefs, or specifications that appear complete at first glance — but contain gaps, contradictions, or ambiguities. For multi-select questions, select ALL issues you can identify. For written questions, list the questions you would ask before starting work. Each question is timed — you will receive a warning before time runs out. You cannot go back to previous questions.

questions.prompt: Review the following requirement and select ALL issues you can identify.
questions.context: Requirement: 'When a customer places an order, send them a confirmation email immediately. If the order total exceeds $500, it must be manually approved by a manager before processing. All orders should be shipped within 24 hours.'

options.text: The confirmation email is sent before manager approval — the customer may think the order is confirmed when it isn't
No definition of what happens if the manager doesn't approve in time to meet the 24-hour shipping window
$500 is too low a threshold for manual approval
No mention of what the confirmation email should contain
The 24-hour shipping commitment conflicts with the manual approval step — approval could take longer than 24 hours
No specification for what happens if the customer's email address is invalid

questions.explanation: The immediate confirmation before approval is misleading (a). There's no timeout or escalation for manager approval (b). The 24-hour shipping guarantee contradicts the open-ended approval process (e). Invalid email handling is missing (f). Option (c) is a business judgment, not a specification gap. Option (d) is a nice-to-have detail, not a functional issue — the requirement says 'confirmation email' which implies standard content.

questions.prompt: Review the following requirement and select ALL issues you can identify.
questions.context: Requirement: 'The employee scheduling system should allow managers to create weekly schedules. Employees can request time off, which managers can approve or deny. The system should prevent scheduling conflicts. Schedules are finalized every Friday for the following week.'

options.text: No definition of what constitutes a 'scheduling conflict'
No deadline for when time-off requests must be submitted relative to the Friday finalization
Weekly schedules are too rigid — some businesses need daily flexibility
No mention of what happens if a manager is unavailable to approve a request before Friday
No specification for how to handle schedule changes after finalization (e.g., sick calls, emergencies)
No mention of notification when a time-off request is approved or denied

questions.explanation: What counts as a conflict is undefined — same time? same role? same department? (a). No request deadline creates chaos near Friday (b). No fallback for unavailable managers creates bottlenecks (d). Post-finalization changes are inevitable and unaddressed (e). Option (c) is a design opinion, not a gap. Option (f) is a reasonable UX concern but not a functional specification issue — the core workflow is intact without it.

questions.prompt: Review the following requirement and select ALL issues you can identify.
questions.context: Requirement: 'Create a customer satisfaction survey that is sent after every completed service call. The survey should use a 1–5 star rating. Results should be shared with the service team weekly. Customers who rate 1 or 2 stars should receive a follow-up call within 24 hours.'

options.text: No definition of what 'completed' means — does the customer confirm completion, or does the agent close the ticket?
A 1–5 star rating is too simplistic — should use a 1–10 scale
No mention of who makes the follow-up call or what they should say
No handling for customers who receive multiple surveys in a short period (e.g., frequent callers)
24-hour follow-up commitment doesn't account for weekends or holidays
No specification for how long the customer has to respond to the survey before the data is considered stale

questions.explanation: The trigger for sending the survey is ambiguous (a). The follow-up call has no owner or script guidance (c). Survey fatigue for frequent callers is unaddressed (d). The 24-hour window ignores non-business days (e). Option (b) is an opinion about scale design, not a gap. Option (f) is a data quality concern but not a functional specification gap — surveys are commonly left open-ended.

questions.prompt: Review the following project brief and select ALL issues you can identify.
questions.context: Project brief: 'We need an automated system to process incoming invoices. Vendors email invoices as PDF attachments. The system should extract the key fields (vendor name, amount, due date), match them to existing purchase orders, and route them to the appropriate department head for approval. Approved invoices are sent to the finance team for payment. The system must process invoices within 4 hours of receipt.'

options.text: No specification for how to handle invoices that don't match any existing purchase order
No mention of what happens when invoices arrive in formats other than PDF (e.g., images, Word docs)
No process for handling duplicate invoices from the same vendor
The 4-hour processing window includes human approval — which the system can't control
No specification for what 'appropriate department head' means — how is routing determined?
PDF extraction is unreliable — this approach won't work
No mention of handling partial matches (e.g., amount on invoice differs from purchase order)

questions.explanation: Unmatched invoices need an exception path (a). Non-PDF formats are likely in practice (b). Duplicate detection is critical for financial systems (c). The 4-hour SLA includes a human step the system can't guarantee (d). Routing logic is unspecified (e). Partial matches (amount discrepancies) are extremely common and need a tolerance or escalation rule (g). Option (f) is a blanket technology judgment, not a specification issue — PDF extraction is a known capability with limitations that can be designed around.

questions.prompt: Review the following requirement and select ALL issues you can identify.
questions.context: Requirement: 'Build a system where company employees can reserve meeting rooms. Rooms are available from 8am to 6pm. Bookings can be made up to 2 weeks in advance. If a room is not checked into within 10 minutes of the reservation start time, the booking is automatically cancelled. Recurring meetings should be supported.'

options.text: No definition of what 'checked in' means — physical sensor, button press, or manual confirmation?
8am to 6pm doesn't specify a timezone — problematic if the company has multiple offices
No mention of maximum booking duration — someone could block a room for the entire day
Recurring meetings with the auto-cancel rule could create problems — a recurring booking could be cancelled every week if the organizer is a few minutes late
No specification for who can cancel or modify someone else's booking
2 weeks in advance is too short for long-term planning
No mention of what happens when a recurring booking conflicts with a one-time booking — which has priority?

questions.explanation: Check-in mechanism is undefined (a). Timezone ambiguity matters for multi-office companies (b). No max duration allows room hoarding (c). Auto-cancel + recurring is a problematic combination (d). Booking modification permissions are missing (e). Priority between recurring and one-time bookings must be defined (g). Option (f) is a business preference, not a specification gap.

questions.prompt: Review the following requirement and select ALL issues you can identify.
questions.context: Requirement: 'Customers can return any product within 30 days of purchase for a full refund. Returns must include the original receipt. Refunds are processed to the original payment method within 5 business days. Items must be in their original packaging.'

options.text: No mention of how to handle returns when the original payment method is no longer valid (e.g., expired card, closed account)
The policy should offer exchanges, not just refunds
No exception handling for defective products — should a defective item still require original packaging?
No specification for whether the 30-day window is from purchase date or delivery date
'Original receipt' is ambiguous — does a digital receipt or bank statement count?
5 business days is too slow for refund processing

questions.explanation: Invalid original payment method is a common edge case (a). Defective products shouldn't require original packaging — this creates a contradiction in the policy (c). Purchase date vs. delivery date matters significantly for shipped items (d). 'Original receipt' needs clarification for digital-first customers (e). Option (b) is a feature suggestion, not a gap. Option (f) is a subjective judgment — 5 business days is industry-standard.

questions.prompt: Review the following project brief and select ALL issues you can identify.
questions.context: Project brief: 'We want to automate our hiring pipeline. Candidates apply through our website. The system should screen resumes against the job requirements, rank candidates by fit, and automatically schedule interviews with the top 10 candidates. Hiring managers receive a daily summary of new applications. The system should respond to every applicant within 48 hours.'

options.text: No definition of how 'fit' is determined or what criteria are used for ranking
Automatically scheduling interviews doesn't account for candidate or interviewer availability
'Top 10' is a fixed number that doesn't scale — a niche role might have 8 applicants total, while a popular role might have 500
The daily summary could be outdated by the time the manager reads it — should be real-time
The 48-hour response requirement doesn't specify what the response contains — rejection, acknowledgment, or next steps?
No mention of bias safeguards in the automated screening and ranking process
No handling for internal candidates or referrals — do they go through the same pipeline?

questions.explanation: Ranking criteria are undefined (a). Auto-scheduling without availability creates conflicts (b). Fixed top-10 doesn't adapt to applicant pool size (c). The 48-hour response content is ambiguous (e). Automated screening without bias safeguards is a legal and ethical risk (f). Internal candidates and referrals may need different handling (g). Option (d) is a preference — daily summaries are standard practice and not inherently flawed.

questions.prompt: What questions would you ask the project owner before starting work on this?
questions.context: Feature brief: 'Build a dashboard that shows real-time sales performance for our regional managers. It should update automatically and be accessible on mobile devices.'

constraints.placeholder_text: List the questions you would ask, and briefly explain why each matters...

criteria: Identifies scope ambiguities (what metrics? which regions? what time ranges?)
Questions data source and freshness (what does 'real-time' mean — seconds? minutes? hourly?)
Asks about access control (which managers see which regions? can they see each other's data?)
Considers edge cases (no data available, partial data, timezone handling across regions)
Asks about existing systems (is there a current solution? what's wrong with it?)
Questions mobile requirements (responsive web? native app? offline access?)

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.sample_strong_response:
Before starting work on this real-time sales dashboard, I'd ask:

1. What decisions will this dashboard drive? 'Real-time sales data' could mean tracking today's revenue vs. target, monitoring individual rep performance, watching for anomalies in transaction patterns, or all of these. The use case determines what metrics matter and what 'real-time' actually means — every 5 seconds? Every 5 minutes? Start-of-day refresh? The infrastructure cost difference is significant.

2. Who is the audience and how will they access it? A dashboard for the VP of Sales on a wall-mounted screen has different design requirements than one for regional managers checking phones between meetings. Device, frequency of use, and technical sophistication all shape the design.

3. What data sources exist and how reliable are they? Do all sales channels (in-store, online, phone) feed into one system? Are there known data quality issues (delayed transactions, duplicate entries, returns not subtracted in real time)? If the data has latency or quality problems, a 'real-time' dashboard will show real-time wrong numbers — which is worse than a delayed dashboard showing correct ones.

4. What does 'real-time' mean for this team specifically? Is there a current report they're comparing against? What's the lag on their current data, and what problem does reducing that lag solve? Sometimes 'we want real-time' actually means 'we want same-day instead of next-week.'

5. Are there comparative benchmarks to display alongside the data? Raw numbers without context (targets, last year's same period, team averages) don't drive decisions — they just display activity.

6. What's the budget and timeline? A real-time data pipeline with streaming updates is a different project than a dashboard that refreshes every 15 minutes from an existing database. The brief doesn't distinguish between these.

questions.prompt: What questions would you ask the project owner before starting work on this?
questions.context: Feature brief: 'We need an alert system that notifies warehouse supervisors when inventory levels for any product fall below the reorder threshold. Alerts should go out immediately via text message.'

constraints.placeholder_text: List the questions you would ask, and briefly explain why each matters...

criteria: Asks who defines reorder thresholds and whether they differ by product or warehouse
Questions what 'immediately' means — is inventory tracked in real-time or updated in batches?
Asks whether supervisors can acknowledge, snooze, or act on alerts directly
Considers alert fatigue — what if dozens of products hit threshold at once?
Asks about escalation — what happens if the supervisor doesn't respond?
Questions whether the alert should include context (current level, reorder quantity, supplier info)
Considers edge cases (new products without thresholds, seasonal inventory fluctuations)

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.sample_strong_response:
Before building this low-stock alert system, I'd ask:

1. What defines 'low stock'? Is there a single threshold for all products, or does it vary? A product that sells 10 units per day needs a different threshold than one that sells 10 per month. A fixed threshold (e.g., 'below 50 units') would trigger constant false alarms for slow-movers and miss genuine stockouts for fast-movers. The reorder lead time matters too — if a supplier takes 2 weeks to deliver, 'low stock' needs to account for the demand during that 2-week window.

2. Who receives the alerts and what are they expected to do? If alerts go to a purchasing team, they need enough context to act (supplier info, reorder quantities, cost). If alerts go to store managers, they may need different information (substitute products, expected restock dates). Alerting the wrong person creates noise without action.

3. How many alerts per day is manageable? If the system generates 200 alerts daily, the team will ignore them. What's the prioritization scheme — alert on everything, or only on high-revenue or high-velocity items? Alert fatigue is a real risk that undermines the system's purpose.

4. What inventory data is available and how accurate is it? If the inventory system doesn't reflect real-time sales, returns, or damages, the alerts will fire on stale data. An alert that says 'low stock' when the shelf is actually full erodes trust immediately.

5. Is this tracking a single warehouse, multiple locations, or both? A product might be low at one store but overstocked at another — does the system need to recommend transfers, or just flag the shortage?

6. What happens when an alert fires — is there an automated reorder workflow, or is this purely informational? If someone has to manually place a reorder for every alert, the system's value depends entirely on how quickly they act.

7. How will we know if the system is working? What metric tells us the alert system actually reduced stockouts or improved reorder timing, versus just generating notifications that nobody acts on?

questions.prompt: What questions would you ask before starting work on this? Focus on gaps, ambiguities, and risks.
questions.context: Project brief: 'We're switching our employee performance reviews from an annual cycle to quarterly reviews. Managers will rate employees on a 1–5 scale across several competencies. Results will be visible to the employee, their manager, and HR. The new process starts next quarter.'

constraints.placeholder_text: List the questions you would ask, and briefly explain why each matters...

criteria: Asks what competencies are being rated and who defines them (role-specific or universal?)
Questions calibration — how is consistency across managers ensured?
Asks about the relationship between reviews and compensation, promotion, or performance improvement actions
Considers the transition — how are existing annual review data and timelines handled?
Asks about employee self-assessment or 360 feedback as part of the process
Questions historical access — can employees see trends across quarters?
Considers the training and communication needed for managers and employees on the new cadence
Questions what 'visible' means — read-only? can employees dispute or comment?
Identifies that the brief describes a solution without stating the problem it solves — questions the root cause

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.sample_strong_response:
Before starting work on this performance review cycle change, I'd ask:

1. What problem is this solving? The brief says to change from annual to quarterly reviews, but doesn't explain why. Are annual reviews producing feedback that's too stale? Are managers and employees finding them unhelpful? Is there a retention problem that faster feedback might address? The reason for the change determines what 'quarterly reviews' should actually look like — if the problem is stale feedback, shorter reviews with more frequent check-ins might work; if the problem is lack of goal alignment, quarterly reviews with structured goal-setting are different.

2. What does a 'quarterly review' consist of? Is it the same full review done 4 times a year (which quadruples the workload) or a lighter check-in with a full review annually? The brief says 'transition to quarterly' but doesn't define the scope of each quarterly cycle. Manager time commitment needs to be realistic.

3. How does this affect compensation and promotion decisions? If annual reviews are tied to salary adjustments or promotion decisions, moving to quarterly reviews raises the question: do compensation decisions happen quarterly too, or does one of the four reviews serve as the compensation review? This has significant HR and budget implications.

4. What's the manager capacity? How many direct reports does each manager have? If a manager has 15 reports and each quarterly review takes 2 hours (prep, meeting, documentation), that's 30 hours per quarter — nearly a full work week. Has anyone assessed whether managers can absorb this?

5. What system or process currently supports annual reviews, and can it handle 4× the volume? If reviews are done in a software system, does licensing or capacity support the increased frequency?

6. What happens to in-progress annual reviews? Employees mid-cycle need a transition plan — do they get an abbreviated annual review first, then switch to quarterly?

questions.prompt: What questions would you ask before starting work on this? Focus on gaps, ambiguities, and risks.
questions.context: Project brief: 'Our company wants to implement a policy where all client-facing documents must be reviewed and approved by a senior team member before being sent out. This applies to proposals, contracts, reports, and presentations. The goal is to improve quality and consistency.'

constraints.placeholder_text: List the questions you would ask, and briefly explain why each matters...

criteria: Asks who qualifies as a 'senior team member' and whether this varies by document type
Questions turnaround time — what is the expected review SLA?
Considers bottleneck risk — what if a single senior person becomes a chokepoint?
Asks what 'reviewed and approved' means — stylistic feedback? accuracy check? legal compliance?
Questions urgency exceptions — what happens when a client needs a document within an hour?
Asks how this is tracked and enforced — honor system or tooling?
Considers the volume impact — how many documents per day/week go to clients?
Questions whether templates or standards exist that could reduce review burden
Identifies that the policy creates a single point of failure and questions the escalation path

levels.label: Exceptional
levels.description: Comprehensive, well-structured, identifies non-obvious issues/steps. Shows systems thinking.

levels.label: Strong
levels.description: Covers key points with good structure. May miss one subtle element but demonstrates clear reasoning.

levels.label: Adequate
levels.description: Identifies obvious issues/steps but lacks depth or misses important considerations.

levels.label: Weak
levels.description: Superficial or partially correct. Shows some reasoning but significant gaps.

levels.label: Poor
levels.description: Misunderstands the task, provides irrelevant response, or demonstrates no structured thinking.

levels.label: No Response
levels.description: Blank or completely off-topic.

questions.sample_strong_response:
Before starting work on this document approval policy, I'd ask:

1. Who qualifies as a 'senior team member'? Is this a specific title, a level, or anyone with enough tenure? If only 2 people qualify, they become a bottleneck. If 20 people qualify, the inconsistency in review standards becomes the problem.

2. What does 'reviewed and approved' mean in practice? Is this a stylistic review (formatting, tone)? A factual accuracy check? A legal/compliance review? Each type requires different expertise and takes different amounts of time. The brief treats all document types the same, but a proposal review is very different from a contract review.

3. What's the expected turnaround time? If a client needs a proposal within 2 hours and the senior reviewer is in meetings all day, what happens? Without an SLA, the policy will either be ignored under time pressure or create delays that damage client relationships. There needs to be an escalation path or delegation mechanism for urgent situations.

4. What's the volume? How many client-facing documents go out per day or week? If the answer is 50 per week and there are 3 senior reviewers, that's 16–17 reviews per person per week — which may be unsustainable alongside their other responsibilities.

5. What constitutes 'client-facing'? Does an informal email to a known client contact count? A Slack message with a data attachment? The boundary between 'client-facing document' and 'routine communication' is unclear, and people will interpret it differently.

6. How is approval tracked and enforced? Is this an honor system or is there tooling? If honor system, compliance will vary wildly by team. If tooling, what's the workflow — email chain? Document management system? Digital signature?

7. What happens if the senior reviewer is unavailable — on leave, out sick, or just unreachable? The policy creates a single point of failure with no fallback defined. Does a peer review suffice? Can a manager authorize an exception?

questions.prompt: Review the following project brief and select ALL issues you can identify.
questions.context: Project brief: 'We want to consolidate our three customer support channels (phone, email, and live chat) into a single ticketing system. All incoming requests become tickets. Tickets are assigned to agents based on availability. Customers receive automatic updates when their ticket status changes. Average response time must be under 2 hours. Agents handle tickets in order of arrival.'

options.text: No priority system — a critical outage ticket and a general question are treated the same under 'order of arrival'
No distinction between response time and resolution time — 'response' could mean an acknowledgment or a solution
Assigning by availability doesn't account for agent specialization — a billing question may go to a technical agent
Live chat has different customer expectations than email — applying a single SLA to both channels may not make sense
The system should use AI to auto-resolve simple tickets
No mention of what happens to in-progress conversations during the transition from the old channels to the new system
No specification for what 'status changes' trigger customer updates — every internal reassignment or only meaningful progress?

questions.explanation: No priority means urgent and trivial are treated identically (a). Response vs. resolution is a critical ambiguity in SLAs (b). Availability-only routing ignores skill matching (c). Different channels have different urgency expectations (d). Transition handling for in-progress conversations is missing (f). Over-notifying on every status change would be disruptive (g). Option (e) is a feature suggestion, not a gap in the current specification.
